title: Nonreciprocal optomechanical entanglement in an asymmetric Fabry-Perot cavity
authors: Jia-Kang Wu, Ning Hu, Jie-Qiao Liao, Xun-Wei Xu
abstract: Nonreciprocal transmission (classical nonreciprocity) in optomechanical systems based on asymmetric Fabry-Perot (F-P) cavities has been theoretically proposed and experimentally demonstrated. However, nonreciprocal quantum effects, particularly nonreciprocal quantum entanglement, remain unexplored in such systems. Here, we propose to generate nonreciprocal optomechanical entanglement in an asymmetric F-P cavity and discuss the connection between the nonreciprocal transmission and nonreciprocal quantum entanglement. We reproduce the nonreciprocal transmission spectra by solving the quantum Langevin equations, and then discuss the optimal parameters to achieve nonreciprocal optomechanical entanglement in the system. We show that a greater and more robust optomechanical entanglement can be approached in the asymmetric F-P cavities, in comparing with the symmetric cavities. Furthermore, we find that the degrees of classical and quantum nonreciprocities do not exhibit positive correlation as expected. Our work shows that the classical and quantum nonreciprocities can be realized simultaneously in the asymmetric F-P cavities, which provide a platform to explore the connection between classical and quantum nonreciprocities.
summary: This paper theoretically investigates nonreciprocal quantum entanglement in asymmetric Fabry-Pérot cavities. By solving the system's dynamics using Quantum Langevin Equations, the authors demonstrate that asymmetry enhances entanglement compared to symmetric setups. The key finding is the realization of simultaneous classical and quantum nonreciprocities without requiring a positive correlation between their respective degrees.
main_problem: The study aims to generate and characterize nonreciprocal quantum entanglement within an asymmetric Fabry-Pérot (F-P) cavity, specifically investigating the relationship between classical and quantum nonreciprocities.
main_result: Asymmetric F-P cavities are shown to enhance both the magnitude and robustness of optomechanical entanglement compared to symmetric counterparts. Crucially, the research demonstrates that classical and quantum nonreciprocities can coexist without exhibiting a positive correlation.
method: The analysis employs solving Quantum Langevin Equations (QLEs) to determine system dynamics, followed by calculating steady-state covariance matrices via the Lyapunov equation for noise propagation.
model_or_system: The physical platform is an optomechanical system realized in an asymmetric F-P cavity. The model uses a Hamiltonian describing light-mechanical coupling and evolves operators using QLEs under approximations like strong driving conditions.
key_observables: Logarithmic negativity (for entanglement), classical nonreciprocity degree ($\eta_c$), quantum nonreciprocity degree ($\eta_q$), and transmission spectra ($T_f$ vs $T_b$).
important_parameters: Asymmetry parameters like reflectivity ($r_L$) and the mechanical spring constant ($K$), as well as driving power ($P$) and temperature ($T$).
main_assumptions: The analysis utilizes approximations such as the strong-driving condition ($\vert\Omega\vert \gg \kappa_{tot}$) to separate mean values from quantum fluctuations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate nonreciprocal transmission spectra dependence on cavity parameters, and show entanglement ($E_{N,\varsigma}$) vs. detuning/power/temperature, comparing asymmetric versus symmetric cavity performance.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the theoretical framework using QLEs to model the system. It then analyzes nonreciprocal transmission spectra, proceeds to quantify quantum entanglement via logarithmic negativity, and finally compares classical and quantum nonreciprocities across various parameter regimes (e.g., varying reflectivity or temperature).
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it pushes beyond simple demonstrations of directional light control by quantifying the *quantum* nature of this directionality (nonreciprocal entanglement) in a well-established platform like optomechanics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of the asymmetric F-P type optomechani- cal cavity, which is formed by a movable mirror (characterized by a reflectivity of rL and a decay rate of κL) and a fixed mir- ror (characterized by a reflectivity of rR and a decay rate of κR).

FIG. 3. Nonreciprocal transmission spectra and logarithmic negativity EN ,ς versus the normalized detuning ∆0/ωm under forward (red) and backward (blue) drivings for different scan- ning directions: (a) and (c) for upward frequency scanning; (b) and (d) for downward frequency scanning. The mechan- ical quality factor Qm = 3.1 × 105, reflectivity rL = 98.1%, spring constant K = 80 N/m, and the temperature T = 10 mK. Other parameters used are the same as Fig. 2.

FIG. 4. Logarithmic negativity EN ,ς versus ∆0 and ωm under various drivings: (a) forward driving and (b) back- ward driving. The red star indicates the peak value of EN ,ς achieved under the forward driving, while the gray area de- notes the unstable region. (c) Logarithmic negativity EN ,ς versus the scaled detuning ∆0/ωm at the mechanical fre- quency ωm/2π = 34.5 MHz. (d) Transmission spectrum ver- sus the scaled detuning ∆0/ωm. (e) Degree of classical non- reciprocity ηc versus quantum nonreciprocity ηq. Other pa- rameters used are: incident power P = 200 mW, reflectivities rL = 96.3% and rR = 99.9%, and temperature T = 10 mK.

FIG. 5. Logarithmic negativity EN ,ς versus ∆0/ωm and re- flectivity rL under either the (a) forward driving or the (b) backward driving. (c) Logarithmic negativity EN ,ς versus re- flectivity rL under the forward and backward drivings. (d) Transmission spectra versus the reflectivity rL under the for- ward (red) and backward (blue) drivings with the normalized detuning ∆0/ωm = 1. (e) Degree of classical nonreciprocity ηc versus quantum nonreciprocity ηq. Other parameters used are the same as those in Fig. 4.

FIG. 6. Logarithmic negativity EN ,ς versus ∆0/ωm and P un- der different drivings: (a) forward driving, (b) backward driv- ing. (c) Logarithmic negativity EN ,ς versus incident power P under the forward (red) and backward (blue) drivings. (d) Transmission spectra versus the incident power P under the forward (red) and backward (blue) drivings. (e) Degree of classical nonreciprocity ηc versus quantum nonreciprocity ηq. The reflectivity of the metasurface is rL = 96.3%. Other pa- rameters used are the same as those in Fig. 5.
